issue_body:
Concerning errors of the form
```bash
self.ml.named_steps = undefined

    208                 coef = (self.ml.named_steps['ml'].coef_ if

issue_title: Couldn't use the CCP test SSO server
issue_body:
While working on an application that used CCP's test SSO server (login.testeveonline.com). I ran into a problem in the EsiSecurity class when verifying the user's authentication status. The ESI api call to "https://esi.tech.ccp.is/verify" didn't include a datasource parameter, the SSO access token (from CCP's test SSO server) was being compared against the tranquility authentication database, which always rejected it as invalid.

I've attached a pull request that should resolve the issue in this instance.

issue_title: Cross-actor tracebacks
issue_body:
When a traceback is thrown in an actor, is there any way to report the original traceback in the calling actor? Debugging is very hard in Pykka as an exception is shown only with the traceback from the calling actor rather than the traceback leading to the actual cause of the exception.

Taking the `counter.py` example, let's assume there's an exception in the `Adder` actor. What we see is:

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "counter.py", line 45, in <module>
    bookkeeper.count_to(10).get()
  File "/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykka/gevent.py", line 47, in get
    return self.async_result.get(timeout=timeout)
  File "/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gevent/event.py", line 385, in get
    return self.get(block=False)
  File "/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gevent/event.py", line 375, in get
    return self._raise_exception()
  File "/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gevent/event.py", line 355, in _raise_exception
    reraise(*self.exc_info)
  File "/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gevent/_util_py2.py", line 8, in reraise
    raise type, value, tb
KeyError: 'arg'
```

What I would love to see is:
```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "counter.py", line 37, in <module>
    raise KeyError('arg')
  File "counter.py", line 35, in <module>
    adder.add_one()
  File "counter.py", line 45, in <module>
    bookkeeper.count_to(10).get()
  File "/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykka/gevent.py", line 47, in get
    return self.async_result.get(timeout=timeout)
  File "/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gevent/event.py", line 385, in get
    return self.get(block=False)
  File "/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gevent/event.py", line 375, in get
    return self._raise_exception()
  File "/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gevent/event.py", line 355, in _raise_exception
    reraise(*self.exc_info)
  File "/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gevent/_util_py2.py", line 8, in reraise
    raise type, value, tb
KeyError: 'arg'
```

Is this somehow possible? Am I missing something, or are cross-border exception tracebacks not possible?

issue_title: Parsing temperatures from string
issue_body: I often want to parse a unit from string, eg from a config file. Normally I would do this like `ureg("1 mm")` or `ureg.Quantity("1 mm")`, which works fine. However, this doesn't work when parsing temperatures. Running `ureg("1 degC")` returns an `OffsetUnitCalculusError`, as does using `Quantity()`. Is there a better way to do this?

issue_title: Unit checking for zero value
issue_body:
It seems like the value `0` is handled specially in some parts of Pint, in the sense that the zero value doesn't always require units. For example, `(1 * ureg.mm) + 0` is valid, yet `(1 * ureg.mm) + 1` is (obviously) not.

So, I'm wondering if this should also be valid (it currently isn't):

```python
@units.check("[length]")
def test(a):
    print(a)

test(0)
```

I'm not entirely sure it should be, but it definitely feels awkward to have to write `test(0 * units.meters)` or something.

issue_title: [patch] Made http.py return ordered headers
issue_body:
_From [alex...@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/110156607140959632921/) on March 28, 2015 16:11:26_

Not a bug, just an improvement.

HTTP headers are returned unordered (they are held in a simple dict). Simple patch to make headers be returned in the same order as they are in the request.

This is helpful for the very particular use case to fingerprint certain browsers.

This does have some drawbacks, since OrderedDicts are python 2.7 and above only. Backwards compatibility can be achieved using ordereddict, but it introduces an extra dependency.

**Attachment:** [ordered_http_headers.patch](http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=138)

_Original issue: http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=138_

issue_title: Timezones in handling date/time
issue_body:
The current handling of times assumes that all the users are in the same timezone as the server will is running on.
Or to put in a different way: if the hipchat user is in a different timezone, methods dealing with time will not work as expected.

Suggested improvement: add timezone support

In mixins/naturaltime.py
```
import pytz

    def parse_natural_time(self, time_str,tz_str="UTC"):
        cal = pdt.Calendar()
        time_tuple = cal.parseDT(datetimeString=time_str, tzinfo=pytz.timezone(tz_str))[0][:-2]

        return datetime.datetime(*time_tuple)
```

In plugins/productivity/remind.py
```
    def remind_somebody_at(self, message, reminder_recipient=None, reminder_text=None, remind_time=None):
        """remind ___ to ___ at ___: Set a reminder for a thing, at a time for somebody else."""

        # get the users timezone from HipChat
		user_details = self.get_hipchat_user(message.sender.hipchat_id)
        user_tz = user_details['timezone']
		# parse the time in the users timezone context
        parsed_time = self.parse_natural_time(remind_time,user_tz)
```

Obviously this depends on the user to having set his timezone properly in HipChat (Account settings - profile).
I am not sure if HipChat servers are smart enough to set this automatically based on the chat clients context, afaik they do not and fall back to UTC as default if the user does not set it manually.
Still this would be useful imho.

Alternatively - to make it super robust - we could also add a helper function that checks the timezone info from HipChat and does a cross-check with message.last_active which is a UTC timestamp and can be compared with now() in UTC.
If there is a delta, this could override the timestamp value from HipChat in case that is returns "UTC" because then obviously it would be the (wrong!) default setting and the user never modified it.

Feedback?

issue_title: showing a AtomGroup loses local changes
issue_body:
When I show a AtomGroup the coordinates are reloaded from the trajectory. If I made any local changes for modeling or analysis they are lost and I can't check if any of my transformation had the desired effect. To reproduce this see code below.

``` python
import MDAnalysis as mda
from MDAnalysisTests.datafiles import PSF, DCD
import nglview as ng

u = mda.Universe(PSF, DCD)
p = u.select_atoms('all')

# change into principle axes frame
pa = p.principle_axes()
p.translate(-p.center_of_geometry())
p.rotate(pa)

p.write('psf_in_pa.pdb')

# this will show the protein with the original loaded coordinates
view = ng.show_mdanalysis(p)
view

# now the protein is shown in the principal axes coordinates system
u2 = mda.Universe('psf_in_pa.pdb')
view2 = ng.show_mdanalysis(u2)
view2
```

I'm aware this is mostly a problem of MDAnalysis and the  current behavior is good to look at a trajectory. To look at a single AtomGroup this isn't ideal though. Adding a new keyword to `show_mdanalysis` to not reload the coordinates would be good solution.

issue_title: Polling loop/thread fragile, gets knocked out by common conditions too easily.
gethostbyname defaults to ipv6
issue_body:
Not sure if it's a bug or intended behaviour, but it's bitten me and other users (probably #22) of `aiohttp` since they [switched to family=0](https://github.com/KeepSafe/aiohttp/pull/561) per default. Most Linux distro's ship with IPv6 enabled (luckily) but most don't have a IPv6 route set up yet. 

Output:

```
Async: ['2a03:b0c0:2:d0::27d:6001']
Socket: ['188.166.29.234', '2a03:b0c0:2:d0::27d:6001']
```

Test:

``` python
import asyncio
import aiodns
import socket

HOST = 'www.vinyl-lp.com'
FAMILY = 0 
PORT = 80

async def aiodns_gethostbyname():
    resolver = aiodns.DNSResolver()
    resp = await resolver.gethostbyname(HOST, FAMILY)
    print("Async: {}".format(resp.addresses))

def regular_gethostbyname():
    resp = socket.getaddrinfo(HOST, PORT, family=FAMILY, proto=socket.IPPROTO_TCP)
    ips = [x[4][0] for x in resp]
    print("Socket: {}".format(ips))


if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(aiodns_gethostbyname())
    loop.close()

    regular_gethostbyname()
```

issue_title: Document the process for configuring logging from a file
issue_body:
Propose using `logging.config.dictConfig` (see https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.config.html for implementation details).

Add a section to the docs for configuring logging from a file. Content should be something like:

```python
import logging
import yaml

def configure_from_file(file_name):
  """
  Convenience function to configure logging from a YAML-format configuration file.
  """

  with open('./logging-config.yml') as logging_config_file:
    logging_config = yaml.load(logging_config_file)

  logging.config.dictConfig(logging_config)
```

Where `logging-config.yml` contains:

```yaml
# Configure logging from scratch.
disable_existing_loggers: True

# Configure the root logger to use Seq
root:
  class: seqlog.structured_logging.StructuredLogger
  level: INFO
  handlers:
    - seq

handlers:
  seq:
    class: seqlog.structured_logging.SeqLogHandler
    formatter: seq

    # Seq-specific settings (add any others you need, they're just kwargs for SeqLogHandler's constructor).
    server_url: 'http://localhost:5341/'
    api_key: 'MyApiKey'

formatters:

```

issue_title: yas3fs deletes the /tmp directory
issue_body:
Hi,

I've discovered a specific set of conditions under which the entire /tmp directory is deleted when yas3fs cleans its cache.

## How to recreate

1. Start with an empty `/tmp` directory.
2. Run yas3fs and add a new file to the mounted directory.
    * The local filesystem cache is created at `/tmp/yas3fs/...`
3. Delete the file you just added to the mounted directory.
    * yas3fs removes that file from its cache
    * empty cache directories are recursively deleted using python's [os.removedirs](https://docs.python.org/2/library/os.html#os.removedirs) method
    * because that was the only file in the cache, the entire `/tmp/yas3fs` directory is deleted
    * **`/tmp` is now empty so it gets deleted**

## Why it happens

This happens because [`FSCache.delete()`](https://github.com/danilop/yas3fs/blob/7b1ee0d8a7ee1577fbe11083fe0b49f1ba5ab24d/yas3fs/__init__.py#L471) calls [`FSData.delete()`](https://github.com/danilop/yas3fs/blob/7b1ee0d8a7ee1577fbe11083fe0b49f1ba5ab24d/yas3fs/__init__.py#L324), which calls [`remove_empty_dirs_for_file()`](https://github.com/danilop/yas3fs/blob/7b1ee0d8a7ee1577fbe11083fe0b49f1ba5ab24d/yas3fs/__init__.py#L3084), which calls [`remove_empty_dirs()`](https://github.com/danilop/yas3fs/blob/7b1ee0d8a7ee1577fbe11083fe0b49f1ba5ab24d/yas3fs/__init__.py#L3055), which uses `os.removedirs()`.

[The docs for os.removedirs()](https://docs.python.org/2/library/os.html#os.removedirs) say that:

> `removedirs()` tries to successively remove every parent directory mentioned in *path* until an error is raised (which is ignored, because it generally means that a parent directory is not empty).

Therefore if `/tmp` is empty after deleting `/tmp/yas3fs`, then it will be deleted too.

## What should happen

yas3fs shouldn't recursively delete beyond its own cache path (i.e. the `--cache-path` option, in this case `/tmp/yas3fs`)

It might be necessary to implement a custom recursive delete function rather than use `os.removedirs()`, since it doesn't look like it's possible to limit that to a specific root directory.

issue_title: XDG.Preference('Gnome') decorator has no effect
issue_body:
The `XDG.Preference(name)` decorator is supposed to multiply priority by 1.5 if `$XDG_CURRENT_DESKTOP` matches the name.

GNOME and Secret Service backends call it with `name = 'Gnome'`.

However, that is never the case. On the mainline GNOME desktop the value is `GNOME` (uppercase), however on desktops derived from GNOME the following values of `$XDG_CURRENT_DESKTOP` are also possible:

* `GNOME-Classic:GNOME` (the semi-official session provided by gnome-shell-extensions package)
* `GNOME-Flashback:GNOME` (the GNOME Flashback session)
* `GNOME-Flashback:Unity` (the GNOME Flashback session on Ubuntu systems)
* `Unity` (Ubuntu's default desktop)
* `X-CINNAMON` (Mint's default desktop)
* …

Even more, the desktops that are not derived from GNOME and don't have their own keyring implementation (i.e. Xfce, LXDE and almost any non-KDE desktop) are usually also using the GNOME Keyring. For example, it is used in Lubuntu (the LXDE Ubuntu flavor).

So we should either update the check to make sure it catches all these variants, or drop it altogether (as it was doing nothing in its current form anyway). I prefer the latter variant.

I start to work on reading field (in general) data from a med file, as you can see [here](https://github.com/tianyikillua/meshio/commit/4106f66d0f1a44bdceaa3ae4082e2f6a0665b825).

Reading nodal field seems easy and should already work.

For reading cell field it may demand some work. The MED format is strongly influenced by Code_Aster, and they in general define "cell field" directly on gauss points. We can adopt the following approach

- for elements where lies only 1 gauss point (linear simplices, or reduced integration), simple (TODO);
- otherwise, we can just perform an arithmetic mean of all the values at the Gauss points inside one particular element.

In the latter case, `meshio` does not only "reading" but also some "computation"...it seems outside its scope. Hence we can also just neglect these fields when the number of Gauss points is larger than 1.

issue_title: scapy.all.sniff sometimes adds 4 bytes of UDP payload
issue_body:
Under the following circumstances :
* UDP over IPv6
* UDP port around 500 (501 works too, but not 600 for example)
* UDP payload length <= 24

scapy.all.sniff incorrectly adds 4 bytes of additional data to the captured UDP payload.

Using a socket to receive the data, or capturing with Wireshark, these 4 bytes do not appear.

Here is a full test case to reproduce (test with 2 interfaces connected by a simple cable):

    #!/usr/bin/env python3

    import contextlib
    import os
    import socket
    import threading
    import unittest

    import scapy.all


    ITF_SRC = "eth6"
    ITF_DST = "eth5"
    IP_SRC = "2014::1"
    IP_DST = "2015::1"
    UDP_PORT = 500


    class SniffThread(threading.Thread):

      def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.args = args
        self.kwargs = kwargs
        self.received = []
        super().__init__()

      def run(self):
        self.received.extend(scapy.all.sniff(*self.args, **self.kwargs))


    class TestScapyUdpCaptureIssue(unittest.TestCase):

      def test_captureSocket(self):
        for udp_payload_size in (25, 24):
          # build udp frame
          eth = scapy.all.Ether(src=scapy.all.get_if_hwaddr(ITF_SRC),
                                dst=scapy.all.get_if_hwaddr(ITF_DST))
          ip = scapy.all.IPv6(src=IP_SRC,
                              dst=IP_DST)
          udp = scapy.all.UDP(sport=UDP_PORT, dport=UDP_PORT)
          payload_udp = os.urandom(udp_payload_size)
          frame = eth / ip / udp / payload_udp

          # listen for future response
          with contextlib.closing(socket.socket(socket.AF_INET6, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)) as sckt_rec:
            sckt_rec.settimeout(1)
            sckt_rec.bind((IP_DST, UDP_PORT))

            # send frame
            scapy.all.sendp(frame, iface=ITF_SRC, verbose=False)

            # check response
            try:
              received, address = sckt_rec.recvfrom(16384)
            except socket.timeout as e:
              self.fail("%s: %s (udp_payload_size = %u)" % (e.__class__.__name__, e, udp_payload_size))
            self.assertEqual(address, (IP_SRC, UDP_PORT, 0, 0), "udp_payload_size = %u" % (udp_payload_size))
            self.assertSequenceEqual(received, payload_udp, "udp_payload_size = %u" % (udp_payload_size))

      def test_captureSniff(self):
        for udp_payload_size in (25, 24):
          # build udp frame
          eth = scapy.all.Ether(src=scapy.all.get_if_hwaddr(ITF_SRC),
                                dst=scapy.all.get_if_hwaddr(ITF_DST))
          ip = scapy.all.IPv6(src=IP_SRC,
                              dst=IP_DST)
          udp = scapy.all.UDP(sport=UDP_PORT, dport=UDP_PORT)
          payload_udp = os.urandom(udp_payload_size)
          frame = eth / ip / udp / payload_udp

          # listen for future response
          ipv6nh_codes = dict(zip(scapy.all.ipv6nh.values(), scapy.all.ipv6nh.keys()))
          sniff_thread = SniffThread(timeout=1,
                                     iface=ITF_DST,
                                     lfilter=lambda x: x[scapy.all.IPv6].nh not in (ipv6nh_codes["ICMPv6"],
                                                                                    ipv6nh_codes["Hop-by-Hop Option Header"]))
          sniff_thread.start()

          # send frame
          scapy.all.sendp(frame, iface=ITF_SRC, verbose=False)
          sniff_thread.join()

          # build expected response
          expected_eth = scapy.all.Ether(src=scapy.all.get_if_hwaddr(ITF_SRC),
                                         dst=scapy.all.get_if_hwaddr(ITF_DST))
          expected_ip = scapy.all.IPv6(src=IP_SRC, dst=IP_DST)
          expected_udp = scapy.all.UDP(sport=UDP_PORT, dport=UDP_PORT)
          expected = expected_eth / expected_ip / expected_udp / payload_udp

          # check response
          self.assertEqual(len(sniff_thread.received), 1, "udp_payload_size = %u" % (udp_payload_size))  # fails if udp_payload_size == 24
          self.assertSequenceEqual(bytes(sniff_thread.received[0]),
                                   bytes(expected),
                                   "udp_payload_size = %u" % (udp_payload_size))


    if __name__ == "__main__":
        unittest.main()

Context :
* Ubuntu 14.04
* Python 3.4
* Scapy v0.13 from this repo.

issue_title: DNS Challenge, Gandi, APIv4 and required delay(s)
issue_body:
I am currently setting up and testing using certbot with lexicon and our Gandi DNS provider.

As is well known if your using the classic i.e. v4 API with Gandi you need to add a sleep 1500 command to give it time to propagate the update to the DNS record.

However I noticed the following -

I am trying to request a cert which has the main common name and also two subject alternative names, this means you need to request three domain txt records and challenges.

I would have expected it to work by sending the request, generating all three _acme-challenge.domain txt records, sleeping for 1500 seconds and then doing the challenge. This would mean just a single 1500 second long delay.

Instead what seems to be happening is that there is a 1500 second delay in between each record creation and challenge resulting in this case in 3 x 1500 seconds of delay i.e. 4500 seconds or 75 minutes! If someone has more subject alternative names the situation would be correspondingly worse.

Would it be possible for the logic of the program to be altered so that all the creations are done in one go as a batch, then there is a single sleep and then the challenges are done in a batch also?

issue_title: object:get, documentation describes the other behavior from lodash
issue_body:
Hello!

Documentation for `get` method differs from the docs for an appropriate method of **lodash**.

[Lodash get](https://lodash.com/docs/4.17.4#get):

```js
var object = { 'a': [{ 'b': { 'c': 3 } }] };
_.get(object, 'a[0].b.c');
// => 3
```

[Pydash get](http://pydash.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#pydash.objects.get):

```python
get({'a': {'b': {'c': [1, 2, 3, 4]}}}, 'a.b.c.1')
# => 2
```

As you could see, list/array access differs (`a[0].b.c` vs `a.b.c.1`). But original lodash syntax works as expected:

```python
get({'a': {'b': {'c': [1, 2, 3, 4]}}}, 'a.b.c[1]')
# => 2
```

The point is to update docs (because it is a little bit confusing now)

Thank you!

We could call it `align` and implement it with similar parameters as the [rmsd](https://github.com/rasbt/biopandas/blob/master/biopandas/pdb/pandas_pdb.py#L118) function. issue_title: Improvement?: Use Lazy Initialization for MDFBlocks
issue_body:
In order to further improve performance on mdf-files with a large header (large number of channels), you could consider using lazy initialization for some mdfblocks (which are not always read anyway).

In my case, runtime was about halved, when using lazy init for:

- CommentBlock
- CCBlock
- CABlock
- ATBlock

I didn't file a pull request for this issue, as I don't know if this has negative effects in some cases.
I setup a branch to show my implementation: [https://github.com/mattispasch/mdfreader/tree/lazy_init_blocks](https://github.com/mattispasch/mdfreader/tree/lazy_init_blocks)

EDIT: My Implementation is not thread-safe!

- Named Entity Recognition 
- Shallow Parsing )

Given current examples, it seems that the code processes everything at once. Is it possible to make things lazy? (i.e. things get annotated / loaded, only if they are requested).

issue_title: Configure autocomplete to only suggest 'public-facing' symbols
issue_body:
Is there a way to configure Jedi's autocomplete to only suggest 'public-facing' symbols from a module / object? Let's say I import the following ```jedi_test``` module:

```py
"""jedi_test module."""

import json

_PRINT_FMT = 'Your json is: "{}""'

def print_json(val):
    """Print a value as json"""
    print(_PRINT_FMT.val)
```
I would only want ```jedi_test.``` to autocomplete to ```jedi_test.print_json```. This would mirror the behavior of the python interpreter ```help()``` function:
```
Help on module jedi_test:

NAME
    jedi_test - jedi_test module.

FUNCTIONS
    print_json(val)
        Print a value as json

FILE
    /Users/zhammer/code/scrap/jedi_test.py
```
Instead, Jedi autocomplete suggests every symbol in the jedi_test namespace, including all dunder methods (ie ```__doc__```), all underscore-prefixed implementation details (ie ```_PRINT_FMT```) and all imported modules (ie ```json```).

![screen shot 2018-04-07 at 7 03 47 pm](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6956487/38461131-70e2991c-3a96-11e8-94f3-8bc48eece5ad.png)

Jedi's [settings documentation](https://jedi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/docs/settings.html) doesn't seem to provide a solution, and I can't find any issues on the matter.

Does anyone know a way to do this? I can't imagine I'm alone in wanting a more concise autocomplete output.

issue_title: Move operation doesn't update cache
issue_body:
### Reproduce steps

1. Mount gdfs in `/gdfs`
2. Create a directory `/gdfs/foo`
3. (optional) Create a file in the new directory (`echo 1 > /gdfs/bar/file.txt`)
4. Create second directory `/gdfs/bar`
5. Move `/gdfs/foo` into `/gdfs/bar`:  `mv /gdfs/foo /gdfs/bar/`


### Expected

Directory is moved and visible at new path: `/gdfs/bar/foo`

### Actual

1. The directory doesn't exist at target path:

```
$ ls /gdfs/bar/foo
ls: cannot access '/gdfs/bar/foo': No such file or directory
```

2. The directory is still visible in the original path.
```
$ ls /gdfs/foo
file.txt
```

issue_title: Websockets code 1006
issue_body:
So I am running a Discord bot on a Digital Ocean Droplet (VM), and am having some problems with the websocket connection.

The bot runs fine, and stays online fine, but every now and again it throws this error:

`
websockets.protocol.fail_connection: Failing the WebSocket connection: 1006
`

You can see a small section of the log [here](https://i.imgur.com/Zz7b1Uc.png) which shows this error is recurring.

It seems that this error should occur from a bad / dodgy internet connection, but I don't think this can be the issue, as Digital Ocean have always maintained that they have very good stability when it comes to things like this ("99.99% Uptime").

You can also see my Droplets network usage [here](https://i.imgur.com/WWBAgWt.png), there are no downspikes during the times in the logs

As far as I can tell this error has no actual effect on my bot, but it is concerning that this occurs this often and I cannot find a way to fix it.

Looking in the [Websockets RFC](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6455#section-7.4.1), 1006 is reserved for abnormal behavior, so does this mean that it could be to do with the Discord API handling things incorrectly (either at their end or the client end)?

Any ideas as to why this is happening / how to stop this error from happening?

issue_title: KafkaProducer.send with explicit key fails to send message to broker
issue_body:
Using kafka-python 1.3.3., Python 2.7.11-1

Brief description of issue:

Sending messages without an explicit key works fine, and results in random partitioning as expected. Sending *with* a string key (where `key_serializer` has been initialized to `str.encode`) results in **no** messages being sent, ever.

Furthermore, attempting to determine what happened to the `send` in the latter case by calling `get` on the object returned by `send` raises a `TypeError` exception, with the message "descriptor 'encode' requires a 'str' object but received a 'unicode'".

Detailed description of issue is here: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45470055/kafka-python-1-3-3-kafkaproducer-send-with-explicit-key-fails-to-send-message-t

issue_title: Records returned by KafkaConsumer.poll() are not the same for each return
issue_body:
I create a topic with one replication factor and three partitions in a Kafka cluster with three servers.

> kafka-topics.sh --describe --zookeeper 10.0.10.10:2181,10.0.10.11:2181,10.0.10.12:2181 --topic test

   Topic: test	Partition: 1	Leader: 1	Replicas: 1	Isr: 1

Then, I create a producer to send messages.

```python
producer = KafkaProducer(bootstrap_servers='10.0.10.10:9092')
for i in range(30):

```

After that, I create a consumer to receive meaasges.

```python

Every time, I run codes below and get different records returned by KafkaConsumer.poll().  Sometimes it returns only one partition's records, sometimes it returns two partitions' records, and other times it returns all records.
Besides that, the order of sending records is disrupted in the KafkaConsumer.poll() results.

Is there any solutions for this? Or could I use other API to do this?

issue_title: Bundle a main method with default command line arguments
issue_body:
Making a little fuzzer app is nicer than manually changing the code every time you want to change an IP address, port number, etc.

The best method I can think of to make app construction easy is to create a sort of default main with some default CLI arguments. Particular protocols can then define more options. Some basic arguments:

```
usage: boofuzz <command>

commands:
    fuzz
    browse-results

fuzz arguments:
    --target              target identifier; format varies by protocol
    --out-csv FILE        output to CSV file
    --out-txt FILE        output to text file
    --sleep-time          time to sleep between test cases
    --restart-sleep-time  time to wait after executing restart
    -d
    --debug               enable debug output on command line
    --fuzz-case NUM       fuzz a particular numbered test case
    --web-port            port number for HTTP GUI
```

`boofuzz fuzz` looks funny, so it would be highly tempting to rename the app `boo`...

issue_title: Disable shell=True for monitoring commands
issue_body:
Hello! This is somewhat related to a [Pull Request I opened](https://github.com/nicolargo/glances/pull/683). I'm checking for security issues in a few projects and noticed that this one uses `shell=True` for checking the output on the monitoring commands. 

```
>> Issue: subprocess call with shell=True identified, security issue.
   Severity: High   Confidence: High
   Location: glances/glances/core/glances_monitor_list.py:138
134             if self.command(i) is not None:
135                 # Execute the user command line
136                 try:
137                     self.__monitor_list[i]['result'] = subprocess.check_output(self.command(i),
138                                                                                shell=True)
```

This could be problematic, especially if the configuration file is not properly sanitized or the command includes some illegal characters. Here's a [Stack Overflow discussion](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/3172470/actual-meaning-of-shell-true-in-subprocess) and a [blog post](http://kevinlondon.com/2015/07/26/dangerous-python-functions.html) related to problems with `shell=True`.

issue_title: Convertbits needs to refuse to pad on decode
issue_body:
The line 

    payload=converted[1:-6]

is wrong, because the checksum is defined to be exactly 40 bits long.  40 bits is 5 bytes, so the correct line is 

    payload=conveted[1:-5]

However, this line also breaks the code because of the fact that there is another bug.  If you look at https://github.com/simon-v/minipos/blob/master/cashaddr.py , you can see that `convertbits` is supposed to avoid padding on the decode stage.  This is why your payload is longer than necessary by one bit.

I have an implementation that is something like a mixture of your implementation and their implementation that fixes these bugs and correctly implements >160 bit lengths at https://github.com/Steve132/wallet_standard/blob/master/python/coffer/coins/_cashaddr.py

issue_title: Perfdata line has one too many ; (semi-colons) per datasource
issue_body:
In the performance data that is output, each datasource has one too many semi-colons.

This:
```
cluster_nodes=2;;;;;
```
Should be:
```
cluster_nodes=2;;;;
```

Some programs discard the performance data because of the last semi-colon. 
The official nagios plugin development guidelines define performance data as:
https://nagios-plugins.org/doc/guidelines.html#AEN200
```
'label'=value[UOM];[warn];[crit];[min];[max]
```

You simply need to change line 295 from:
```
return ("%s=%s%s;%s;%s;%s;%s;" %
```
To:
```
return ("%s=%s%s;%s;%s;%s;%s" %
```

Keep up the great work!

Originally reported here:
https://support.nagios.com/forum/viewtopic.php?f=38&t=34984&p=162683#p162573

User selected columns to filter

I need some ideas, or maybe some best practices on how I should implement this feature.

Basically, if there is an external filter which is set to `data-column="all"` ([demo](http://jsfiddle.net/abkNM/4367/)), I want the user to be able to specify which column(s) they want to search.

These are some ideas on how a user could specify the desired column when searching for "abc" in the first column AND "10" in the second:

* `0:abc && 1:10`
* `AlphaNumeric:abc && Numeric:10` (column titles will likely be case insensitive)

Currently if you search for "abc && 10" in the demo, you get 3 results, one of which includes "ABC 10" in the first column with "87" in the second.

issue_title: Argument parsing fails with --option=equals_delimiter and a config file
issue_body:
## Test Files

`test.py`
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import argparse
from pprint import pprint

import configargparse

a = argparse.ArgumentParser()
a.add_argument("--config")
a.add_argument("--foo")
args = a.parse_args()
pprint(args)

p = configargparse.getArgumentParser()
p.add_argument("--config", is_config_file=True)
p.add_argument("--foo")
args = p.parse_args()
pprint(args)
```

`test.ini`
```ini
foo=VALUE_IN_CONFIG
```

## Example of Issue

Using `--foo=bar` (with equals) works without config parsing:
```
./test.py --foo=bar
Namespace(config=None, foo='bar')
Namespace(config=None, foo='bar')
```

However it breaks with config parsing (but not for --config):
```
./test.py --foo=bar --config=test.ini
Namespace(config='test.ini', foo='bar')
Namespace(config='test.ini', foo='VALUE_IN_CONFIG')
```

Using `--foo bar` (with space instead of equals) shows us the expected behavior:
```
./test.py --foo bar --config test.ini
Namespace(config='test.ini', foo='bar')
Namespace(config='test.ini', foo='bar')
```

issue_title: The multiline_indicator allows comments after a line-continuating backslash
issue_body:
The multiline_indicator regex is `'\\\\(\s*#.*)?\n'`. This would allow a comment after a backslash used for explicit line joining, like:
```python
if a == b \  # a equals b
        and b == c:
    pass
```
The above is not valid python syntax:
> A line ending in a backslash cannot carry a comment.
> - https://docs.python.org/3/reference/lexical_analysis.html#explicit-line-joining

Even worse, the multiline_indicator regex also sees a line continuation with comment in the following regex from [HamlPy](https://github.com/jessemiller/HamlPy/blob/master/hamlpy/elements.py#L16):
```python
'(?P<id>\#[\w-]*)?'
```

This breaks minifying code with similar regexes.

I think the multiline_indicator should only look for lines ending in only a backslash (and perhaps some whitespace).

issue_title: Output coroutine functions
issue_body:
It would be great if `@decorator` could be used to create decorators which produce [coroutine functions](https://docs.python.org/3/library/inspect.html#inspect.iscoroutinefunction).  Is there already such functionality and I just didn't find it? If not, would this be feasible? If so, are there any plans for this? Would you accept PRs?

Example:
```python
from inspect import iscoroutinefunction
from decorator import decorator

@decorator
async def ensure_coro(function, *args, **kwargs): 
    ret = function(*args, **kwargs)
    if iscoroutinefunction(function):
        ret = await ret
    return ret

@ensure_coro
def foo():
    return 42

assert iscoroutinefunction(foo)
```

issue_body:
### Environment

To diagnose, we usually need to know the following, including version numbers.  On Windows, be
sure to specify 32-bit Python or 64-bit: Windows 64-bit

- Python: 3.6.1
- pyodbc: 4.0.23
- OS: Windows
- DB: NA
- driver: NA

### Issue

I am working with a connection to hadoop that requires MANY arguments to set all of the parameters correctly.  When I create this connection string, I get an error when using pyodbc.connect() that says 'connection string too long'.   

As an attempt to debug, I tried changing line 66 of the /src/connection.cpp file to
`const int cchMax = 5000;`
and reinstall the package using `python setup.py install', but this did not fix the issue.  (I admit that I am very naive when it comes to c++)

I have tried alternative approaches to constructing my connection string but have ran into other errors as well.  For instance:
```
pyodbc.connect("Driver={Hortonworks Hive ODBC Driver}",
"allowselfsignedservercert=1",
"applysspwithqueries=1",
"asyncexecpollinterval=100",...)
```
returns 'function takes at most 1 non-keyword argument'.  Another approach was:
```
pyodbc.connect(Driver="{Hortonworks Hive ODBC Driver}",
allowselfsignedservercert="1",
applysspwithqueries="1",
asyncexecpollinterval="100",
ssp_hive.execution.engine="tez", ...)
```

but this returned 'keyword can't be an expression'.

Ideally, if I could pass this entire connection string as a single string and not hit the length limit, I think that would be my most ideal situation.

Any help is greatly appreciated!

P.S. I have tried my connection string in other languages and have successfully connected so I do not believe there is an issue with my connection string contents.

issue_title: Fortran Best Practices
issue_body:
It would be nice to have a module `bp.py` and accompanying script that allow a user to analyze their library from a best practices point of view. For example, we just uncovered this super-subtle error:

- For pointers, `associated` returns `T` in `gfortran` for pointer arrays in user-derived types when the pointer to the array is still undefined (i.e. has not yet been nullified or set to a target). When a new user-derived type is created, pointer arrays should *always* be set to `null()` as a best practice.
- For subroutines that have parameters with `intent(out)` and a user-derived type, the subroutine *must* set the value of *all* the members in the `intent(out)` parameter. If any of those members are pointer arrays, their values will be *undefined* if they are not explicitly set to `null()` (or some other existing target) in the subroutine. This is a subtlety with `intent(out)` parameters.

These two conditions could turn up from a best-practices search by `fortpy`. It wouldn't be hard at all to include a basic search of parameters and default values for user-derived types.

issue_title: Ragged Arrays of Complex User Types
issue_body:
It turns out there is another, _really_ nasty use case for the automated unit testing that isn't handled yet. Suppose I have a derived type such as:

```fortran
type nasty
  integer :: A(:,:)
  logical :: B(:)
  complex :: C(:,:,:)
end type nasty
```

Next, suppose that the subroutine I am unit testing uses a 3D array of these guys:

```fortran
subroutine uses_nasty(narray, oarray)
  type(nasty), intent(in) :: narray(:,:,:)
  type(nasty), intent(out) :: oarray(:,:)
end subroutine uses_nasty
```

To initialize the input parameter, we would need a bunch of files storing the arrays. For example `A.in.{}`, `B.in.{}` and `C.in.{}`. Since `C` is already 3D, there is no hope of doing this using a ragged array setup. Instead, we could use `<part>` tags to nest the assignment of the values to these members of the derived type `nasty`.

```xml
<assignment name="narray" value="p1" allocate="10,5,2">
  <value identifier="A" file="A.in.{}.*" folder="./tests" member="A" suffix="$1.$2.$3" rename="A.in" />
  <value identifier="B" file="B.in.{}.*" folder="./tests" member="B" suffix="$1.$2.$3" rename="B.in" />
  <value identifier="C" file="C.in.{}.*" folder="./tests" member="C" suffix="$1.$2.$3" rename="C.in" />
  <part identifier="p1">
    <part identifier="p2">
      <part identifier="p3" value="A, B, C" />
    </part>
  </part>
</assignment>
```

The new _suffix_ attribute specifies the text (where loop variable values are substituted for the `$i` values) to replace `*` with in the file name when searching for the source to load a variable's value from. When the driver is generated, a call to `fpy_period_join_indices` will generate the suffix and it will be spliced in by python in the right place using `filename.replace("*", "// varname_fpy_suffix //")`. The wildcard in the filename will still ensure that the existing routines will copy all the necessary files to the execution directory for a given test case. The above XML says to allocate `narray(10,5,2)` and then create a loop system:

```fortran
do narray_fpy_1=1, size(narray, 1)
  do narray_fpy_2=1, size(narray, 2)
    do narray_fpy_3=1, size(narray, 3)
      call fpy_period_join_indices(narray_fpy_suffix, (/ narray_fpy_1, narray_fpy_2, narray_fpy_3 /), 3)
      call fpy_read(narray(narray_fpy_1, narray_fpy_2, narray_fpy_3)%A, "A.in." // narray_fpy_suffix)
      call fpy_read(narray(narray_fpy_1, narray_fpy_2, narray_fpy_3)%B, "B.in." // narray_fpy_suffix)
      call fpy_read(narray(narray_fpy_1, narray_fpy_2, narray_fpy_3)%C, "C.in." // narray_fpy_suffix)
    end do
  end do
end do
```

Things to do:

1. Add support for the _suffix_ attribute to the `<value>` tags. Also the _member_ attribute is still awaiting implementation.
2. Replace the `*` with the appropriate suffix code (which includes initializing a new suffix variable and calling `fpy_period_join_indices`) in all calls to `fpy_read`.
3. Allow a comma-separated list of `<value>` identifiers to be specified for `<part>` _and_ `<assignment>` tags. The order they appear in the comma-separated list determines the order in which they are executed. Thus, a user could assign values to `A, B, C` above using those `<value>` tags and then still call an embedded function (for example).

issue_title: Add/document support for tracking action failures
issue_body:
Currently there seems to be no way to track if the action actually succeeds. This makes it possible for the bot to continue repeating actions that will continuously fail.

One way to handle this would be to allow passing a optional `on_error` callback to `self.do(...)` function. This would allow easier debugging for cases when you continuously fail to apply an upgrade to building, for example.

issue_title: how to install dot command
argcomplete + configargparse: adding certain types of config directives to config files breaks tab-completion
issue_body:
I **LOVE** argcomplete. I also love [configargparse](https://github.com/bw2/ConfigArgParse), a drop-in replacement for the standard argparse which allows you to integrate config files & environment variables with standard cmdline opts.

Thankfully they work together almost flawlessly.
Almost because when I put certain types of options in a config file argcomplete totally fails. It doesn't complete ANYTHING.

**Here's a reproducer:**

```
$ cat koalaberz.py 
#!/usr/bin/python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
# PYTHON_ARGCOMPLETE_OK
try:
    import configargparse
except:
    print("Missing configargparse; Run:\n  pip install configargparse\n")
    raise
try:
    import argcomplete
except:
    print("Missing argcomplete; Run:\n  pip install argcomplete; activate-global-python-argcomplete\n")
    raise

p = configargparse.ArgParser(default_config_files=['koala1.cfg','koala2.cfg'])
p.add('--togglefoo', action='store_true', help='Turn on the foo')
p.add('--appendbar', action='append', metavar='THINGS', help='Add things to bar')
p.add('--pickbaz', choices=['a', 'b', 'c'], help='Choose your baz')
argcomplete.autocomplete(p)
opts = p.parse_args()
print(opts)

$ chmod +x koalaberz.py 

$ ./koalaberz.py -h
usage: koalaberz.py [-h] [--togglefoo] [--appendbar THINGS]
                    [--pickbaz {a,b,c}]

Args that start with '--' (eg. --togglefoo) can also be set in a config file
(koala1.cfg or koala2.cfg or ) by using .ini or .yaml-style syntax (eg.
togglefoo=value). If an arg is specified in more than one place, then command-
line values override config file values which override defaults.

optional arguments:
  -h, --help          show this help message and exit
  --togglefoo         Turn on the foo
  --appendbar THINGS  Add things to bar
  --pickbaz {a,b,c}   Choose your baz
```

**Look good? Now a quick demo that the tab-completion works.**

```
$ ./koalaberz.py -                                                           <--- Pressed Tab here
--appendbar  -h           --help       --pickbaz    --togglefoo  
$ ./koalaberz.py --pickbaz                                                   <--- Pressed Tab here
a  b  c  
$ ./koalaberz.py --pickbaz a --togglefoo -                                   <--- Pressed Tab here
--appendbar  -h           --help       --pickbaz    --togglefoo  
$ ./koalaberz.py --pickbaz a --togglefoo --appendbar junk --appendbar more   <--- Pressed Enter here
Namespace(appendbar=['junk', 'more'], pickbaz='a', togglefoo=True)
```

**When I add config directives of type `store`, all is well.**

```
$ echo pickbaz = b >koala1.cfg

$ ./koalaberz.py -                                                    <--- Pressed Tab here
--appendbar  -h           --help       --pickbaz    --togglefoo  

$ ./koalaberz.py                                                      <--- Pressed Enter here
Namespace(appendbar=None, pickbaz='b', togglefoo=False)
```

**But watch what happens when I add a boolean:**

```
$ echo togglefoo = true >koala2.cfg

$ ./koalaberz.py                                                      <--- Pressed Tab here
.bash_history  .bashrc        .config/       koalaberz.py   .ssh/   
.bash_logout   bin/           koala1.cfg     .lesshst       .viminfo
.bash_profile  .cache/        koala2.cfg     repro          .vimrc  

$ ./koalaberz.py                                                      <--- Pressed Enter here
Namespace(appendbar=None, pickbaz='b', togglefoo=True)
```

**So adding a boolean toggle to a config file breaks argcomplete for some reason. ??? Note that configargparse lets you specify booleans in multiple ways, but none of them work with argcomplete.**

```
$ echo togglefoo >koala2.cfg
$ ./koalaberz.py 
Namespace(appendbar=None, pickbaz='b', togglefoo=True)

$ echo --togglefoo >koala2.cfg
$ ./koalaberz.py 
Namespace(appendbar=None, pickbaz='b', togglefoo=True)

$ echo togglefoo: true  >koala2.cfg
$ ./koalaberz.py 
Namespace(appendbar=None, pickbaz='b', togglefoo=True)
```

**Another oddity is that options of type `append` work as long as I don't actually store more than one value in the list.**

```
$ echo appendbar = barbar >koala2.cfg
$ ./koalaberz.py
Namespace(appendbar=['barbar'], pickbaz='b', togglefoo=False)
$ ./koalaberz.py -
--appendbar  -h           --help       --pickbaz    --togglefoo  

$ echo appendbar = [bar 1, bar 2] >koala2.cfg
$ ./koalaberz.py
Namespace(appendbar=['bar 1', 'bar 2'], pickbaz='b', togglefoo=False)
$ ./koalaberz.py                                                      <--- Pressed Tab here
.bash_history  .bashrc        .config/       koalaberz.py   .ssh/   
.bash_logout   bin/           koala1.cfg     .lesshst       .viminfo
.bash_profile  .cache/        koala2.cfg     repro          .vimrc  
```

Given how awesome both of these modules work together ... and how I hope they'll be in the standard library someday, I'd love to see this fixed, but of course it's an odd problem ... what do you think? Knowing the argcomplete code like you do ... do you have any ideas of what's causing this?

Thanks for reading,
Ryan

issue_title: Simplify programmatically sending invitations
issue_body:
I'm working on an application that has `USER_ENABLE_INVITATION` and `USER_REQUIRE_INVITATION` set. In the initial database, there are no users and an elegant way to bootstrap that would seem to run a flask cli command to send an initial invitation.

With the code shown below, I can send an invitation as follows:

    SERVER_NAME=localhost:8000 FLASK_APP=app flask invite info@example.org

However, the below code is quite long and mostly copy-pasted from the `views.invite` function. It would probably be more elegant if `UserManager` exposed an `invite()` method (or something like that), that could be used from the application as well as from `views.invite`.

Here's what I'm using now:

```
@app.cli.command('invite')
@click.argument('email')
def initdb_command(email):
    """Invite a user to this project."""
    user_invite = db_adapter.add_object(
                db_adapter.UserInvitationClass,                                
                    email = email,                                             
                    invited_by_user_id = -1,                                   
                )                                                              
    db_adapter.commit()                                                        
    user_invite.token = user_manager.generate_token(user_invite.id)            
    db_adapter.commit()

    accept_invite_link = flask.url_for('user.register', token=user_invite.token, _external=True)
                                                                               
    try:
        # Send 'invite' email                                                  
        flask_user.emails.send_invite_email(user_invite, accept_invite_link)   
    except Exception as e:
        # delete new User object if send fails
        db_adapter.delete_object(user_invite)
        db_adapter.commit()    
        raise
    
    print('Sent invite to ' + email)  
```

issue_title: [Enhancement?] Empyrical library

Hi,

I am reviewing the method to calculate drawdown and maybe it is a good idea to use the formula of empyrical library. Also others ratio could be added such as calmar ratio....

http://quantopian.github.io/empyrical/_modules/empyrical/stats.html



def max_drawdown(returns):
 if len(returns) < 1:
        return np.nan

    cumulative = cum_returns(returns, starting_value=100)
    max_return = cumulative.cummax()
    return cumulative.sub(max_return).div(max_return).min()

where cum_returns(returns):
Notes
    -----
    For increased numerical accuracy, convert input to log returns
    where it is possible to sum instead of multiplying.
    PI((1+r_i)) - 1 = exp(ln(PI(1+r_i)))     # x = exp(ln(x))
                    = exp(SIGMA(ln(1+r_i))   # ln(a*b) = ln(a) + ln(b)
df_cum = np.exp(np.log1p(returns).cumsum())

Rgds,
JJ

issue_title: Proposals for pyresttest v2
issue_body:
I am currently considering changes for the next version of PyRestTest (version 2).   In order to extend the framework, I would like to make some design changes, which may break back compatibility (generally in small ways). 

Because of this, I'm throwing this out for community/user comment.  Please comment on this issue, to weigh in with your thoughts and if one of these would cause problems for your uses. 

##Proposed changes:

1. Replace the Command line arguments / TestConfig / Test / Benchmark configuration with a nested set of configuration scope objects.  This may take place over a couple releases, but should enable greater control of test configuration, and simplify adding new options.  **Con:  may break some pure-Python users.** 
2. **Replace explicit templating with more powerful implict templating, using a library.**  This makes the syntax cleaner and more powerful, and greatly simplifies logic for generating content.  As an intermediate step, I may continue to support existing template syntax where currently allowed.  **Con: you will need to escape special characters.   May also be slightly slower, depending.**  Template syntax will probably be Jinja2. 
3. **Remove implict type conversions in YAML test parser.**  Yes, it's beautiful that it tries to implicitly fix things.  However, this makes the parsing code far more complex than needed, and rather hard to add new features and options.   Con: if you're relying on this feature, tests break.  
4. **Reach goal: execution phases.**  The idea here is to refactor the run_test, run_benchmark, and run_testsets methods into discrete steps.  This allows for much easier enhancements, and may constitute a new extension point.  End goal is to enable load testing, parallel execution, and much easier extensions of these steps. 

##Notes:
Templating performance comparison:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1324238/what-is-the-fastest-template-system-for-python

issue_title: Profiling / Optimization - to do list
issue_body:
I'm now in the processing of profiling the code, and comparing run-speeds to FORTRAN. I'll note in this issue aspects of the code which are too slow, and how we can speed them up.

Convolution via frame convolver:

ORIGINAL:

psf = 21x21

lsst_solution: 0.7838027477264404
euclid_solution: 2.7468605041503906
hst_solution: 9.063872337341309
hst_up_solution: 20.911508083343506

psf = 41x41

lsst_solution: 3.622267246246338
euclid_solution: 10.063962459564209
hst_solution: 36.11542248725891
hst_up_solution: 121.993239402771

JITTED:

psf = 21x21

lsst_solution: 0.03985404968261719
euclid_solution: 0.09821200370788574
hst_solution: 0.30489659309387207
hst_up_solution: 0.7619879245758057

psf = 41x41

lsst_solution: 0.07627749443054199
euclid_solution: 0.1665787696838379
hst_solution: 0.45037412643432617 ( FORTRAN = 0.04)
hst_up_solution: 1.0230822563171387 (FORTRAN = 0.096)

NOTES:

- Numba is key to speeding convolution up to reasonable run-times.
- The frame convolver is x10 slower than FORTRAN - and is giving run-speeds that will be problematic for a realistic lens analysis.

The frame convolver should not be slower than FORTRAN after jitting - as the FORTRAN code includes the process of mapping the image to 2D for convolution and reducing it back to 1D.

The reason the frame convolver is slower is because of its large number of -1 entries, which represents pixel in the frame that are outside of the masked region. The code reads every -1 and then continues onto the next index. Below is the fraction of values for each frame array that are -1s:

LSST:

frame_array: 0.4806965585227189
buring_frame_array: 0.8761812348231439

Euclid

frame_array: 0.24736280724621756
buring_frame_array: 0.8446561570493754

HST:

frame_array: 0.12451287733922718
buring_frame_array: 0.826131601559918

HST_up:

frame_array: 0.07482219308446714
buring_frame_array: 0.8173658286983688

Therefore, we need a new data representation that removes all -1's, albeit we need to think carefully about memory. 

I suggest that we remove all of the -1's and store a new list of arrays which represent, for every element in frame_array / blurring_frame_array, the index of the kernel they map too. Then, instead of iterating over the length of the kernel in the frame convolver, we would iterative over the length of each frame_array / blurring_frame_array and grab the kernel index using the new array.

Currently frame_array and blurring_frame_array are lists of numpy arrays. This new scheme would allow us to make them one giant NumPy array. Numba might be happier if we did this, but it could produce a hit on memory?

This problem will also effect the pixelization matrix convolution and be solved in the same manner!

issue_title: Deadlock in send_message in socket_client.py
issue_body:
When send_message is called with wait_for_response set to True, the callback is registered after the message is sent.
This means there's a risk of wait_for_response never returning if the message reply has already been received before the callback is registered.

A proposed patch is included below.

Patch:
```
diff --git a/pychromecast/socket_client.py b/pychromecast/socket_client.py
index ee76f9e..5655f3f 100644
--- a/pychromecast/socket_client.py
+++ b/pychromecast/socket_client.py
@@ -550,8 +550,14 @@ class SocketClient(threading.Thread):
             raise PyChromecastStopped("Socket client's thread is stopped.")
         if not self.connecting and not self._force_recon:
             try:
+                callback = self._request_callbacks[request_id] = {
+                    'event': threading.Event(),
+                    'response': None,
+                }
                 self.socket.sendall(be_size + msg.SerializeToString())
             except socket.error:
+                del self._request_callbacks[request_id]
+                wait_for_response = False
                 self._force_recon = True
                 self.logger.info('Error writing to socket.')
         else:
@@ -559,10 +565,6 @@ class SocketClient(threading.Thread):

         response = None
         if not no_add_request_id and wait_for_response:
-            callback = self._request_callbacks[request_id] = {
-                'event': threading.Event(),
-                'response': None,
-            }
             callback['event'].wait()
             response = callback.get('response')
         return response
```

issue_title: Bug: parse multiple metadata tags
issue_body:
Starting from 08 August 2017 ET is adding new `metadata` to its rules, ([as stated herre](http://lists.emergingthreats.net/pipermail/emerging-sigs/2017-June/028148.html)).

This is a big help for developers and analists, but there are some problem with the rule parser: if there are more than 1 `metadata` tag in the raw rule only the last will be kept by the parser. This because the code does not assume that anyone would use more than 1 `metadata` tag in each rule ([code here](https://github.com/jasonish/py-idstools/blob/master/idstools/rule.py#L261)).

However some rule now has 2 `metadata` tags, ie:
```
alert udp $HOME_NET any -> any 53 (msg:"ET TROJAN CryptON/Nemesis/X3M Ransomware Onion Domain"; content:"|01 00 00 01 00 00 00 00 00 00|"; depth:10; offset:2; content:"|10|yvvu3fqglfceuzfu"; fast_pattern; distance:0; nocase; metadata: former_category TROJAN; reference:url,blog.emsisoft.com/2017/05/01/remove-cry128-ransomware-with-emsisofts-free-decrypter/; reference:url,www.cyber.nj.gov/threat-profiles/ransomware-variants/crypt-on; classtype:trojan-activity; sid:2024525; rev:2; metadata:affected_product Windows_XP_Vista_7_8_10_Server_32_64_Bit, attack_target Client_Endpoint, deployment Perimeter, tag Ransomware_Onion_Domain, tag Ransomware, signature_severity Major, created_at 2017_08_08, malware_family Crypton, malware_family Nemesis, performance_impact Low, updated_at 2017_08_08;)
```

This rule has these metadata:
```
metadata: former_category TROJAN; 
```
```
metadata:affected_product Windows_XP_Vista_7_8_10_Server_32_64_Bit, attack_target Client_Endpoint, deployment Perimeter, tag Ransomware_Onion_Domain, tag Ransomware, signature_severity Major, created_at 2017_08_08, malware_family Crypton, malware_family Nemesis, performance_impact Low, updated_at 2017_08_08;
```

As stated in [SNORT rule manual](http://manual-snort-org.s3-website-us-east-1.amazonaws.com/node31.html#Snort_Metadata_Keys) and in [Suricata sources](https://github.com/inliniac/suricata/blob/e6df113e234c7db7b0e893a25625d981bf09d3f4/src/detect-metadata.c#L38), `metadata` are ignored by IDS/IPS engine. Also examples states that a writer can use more than one `metadata` tag.

The right behaviour would be to _append_ any new `metadata` list to the existing one.

issue_title: Resolve .has_value, .has_default, .has_values and value proxy inconsistency
issue_body:
We are paying too much attention to items with no default value. Those are basically `required` in other libraries. This makes it pretty obvious why getting values will fail if this config does not have a value:

```
item = ConfigItem('a', 'b', required=True)
```

A required config is not something that a program can run without, so it's ok to blow up.

So, todo:
 * [x] if default is not set, so be it, and return it as it is -- do not throw value missing exceptions.
 * [x] add new attribute (via descriptor) - `.required` of type `boolean` which defaults to `False`.
 * [x] rename `ConfigValueNotSet` to `ConfigValueMissing`
 * [x] raise `ConfigValueMissing` when a required config item value is not set (and the item has no default).
 * [x] ConfigValueProxy should return all values, not just ones that are set. User should be using ConfigItemProxy if they want to inspect the nature of config items and pick only those that have user defined values.
 * [x] Keep `has_value`, `has_default` intact.
 * [x] Remove `.has_values` property from `ConfigManager`. Replace with `.is_default` which should return `True` if item has no value set or the value set equals the default value (that is NOT the opposite of what `.has_values` was returning).
 * [x] Remove `.has_values` property from `ConfigSectionProxy`, replace with `.is_default` as above

----

Outdated discussion:

What does having a value mean?

a. it means to have a custom value
b. it means that either a default or a custom value is set, so calling `.value` won't raise an exception

Who does what?
 - `ConfigItem.has_value` (a)
 - `ConfigManager.has_values` (a)
 - `ConfigSectionProxy.has_values` (a)
 - `ConfigValueProxy` returns items which meet (b)

Solution:
 - Rename `.has_value` to `.has_custom_value` (or something else) and make a new `.has_value` which behaves as in (b)
 - Perhaps add `.has_custom_values`? Too long a name!

Other Ideas:
 - Implement `ConfigItem.is_default` -- returns `not ConfigItem.has_value`

issue_title: High memory consumption after long uptime
issue_body:
I'm on bitcoin (segwit) mainnet, running https://github.com/kyuupichan/electrumx/commit/0dc99d7c63f2fe8e4e6f758afddc9024a239c3ff

I have noticed that e-x is using a significant amount of RAM, currently 10.75 GiB.
```
electrumx_rpc.py getinfo
{
    "closing": 1556,
    "daemon": "172.19.1.10:8332/",
    "daemon_height": 530351,
    "db_height": 530351,
    "errors": 849,
    "groups": 178,
    "logged": 0,
    "paused": 0,
    "peers": {
        "bad": 0,
        "good": 96,
        "never": 1,
        "stale": 8,
        "total": 105
    },
    "pid": 1,
    "requests": 1,
    "sessions": 1688,
    "subs": 16777,
    "txs_sent": 27448,
    "uptime": "84d 06h 51m"
}
```
(actually my server was having temporary connection issues, and in the preceding ~10 minutes of this getinfo call, the session count dropped from approx 2500; but probably not relevant. the memory usage did not change.)

I had originally noticed about a month ago, and from what I said on IRC (as random remark), another data point:
```
how much RAM does e-x normally use? it's at 6.6 GB for me now. 2k sessions, 43k subs, 55 days uptime
```
However, while looking at this, @JustinTArthur made me realise I'm on a relatively old version of CPython..:
```
Python 3.6.1 (default, May 11 2017, 22:14:44)
[GCC 4.9.2] on linux
```
It's from when I first built my docker image for electrumx; turns out every time I rebuilt the image, it reused the local `python:3` image instead of updating (stupid default behaviour imho, but oh well).

issue_title: This platform is not supported
issue_body:
I directly imported pystray after installing with pip3
> ImportError: this platform is not supported

I'm currently on ubuntu.
Doing a little digging, I went through the imports one by one and found Xlib is not installed.

`pip3 install python3-xlib`

ah... I think I see how this is a problem.
Since python-xlib does not install on python3,
and python3-xlib does not installl on python2,
trying to create a universal pystray will fail since you can't require the same type of packages.

I think getting a more informative error (in case an import fails) would be useful.

issue_title: Storing unicode keys returns bytestrings that must be decoded as utf8.
issue_body:
I know sqlite is utf-8 internally, that's fine.

But I end up with boiler plate code that says:
- for dictionaries of keys of str, decode as utf-8 -- on retrieval, only.

This seems wrong.  Shouldn't sqlitedict do this for us?

```
>>> mydict = sqlitedict.SqliteDict('/tmp/some_.db', autocommit=True)
>>> hamsterface = u'🐹'
>>> print hamsterface, repr(hamsterface), type(hamsterface)
🐹 u'\U0001f439' <type 'unicode'>
>>> mydict[hamsterface] = {hamsterface: hamsterface}
>>> print mydict.keys()[0], repr(mydict.keys()[0]), type(mydict.keys()[0])
🐹 '\xf0\x9f\x90\xb9' <type 'str'>
>>> ## Here, it is raw bytes, not unicode.  It still "prints" because my terminal happens to also be utf-8
>>> mydict.values()[0]
{u'\U0001f439': u'\U0001f439'}
>>> ## Yet, the value remains unicode. The only way to get the same unicode dict that we tried to represent back, is to decode it manually.
>>> decoded = { mydict.keys()[0].decode('utf-8'): mydict.values()[0] }
>>> decoded
{u'\U0001f439': {u'\U0001f439': u'\U0001f439'}}
```

If you agree, that the .keys() and .items() and so on should automatically decode str-type as utf-8 into unicode-type, then I will be happy to submit a PR.

issue_title: Relative path for plugin folder
issue_body:
Hello!

Plugin folder is located outside of flask root folder because I plan my app will have many extensions. But `flask-plugins` search folder in flask root folder only.

My suggestion ([see Stackoverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/a/17295128/6105514)): In file `__init__.py` change line 240 from:

```
self.plugin_folder = os.path.join(self.app.root_path, plugin_folder)
```
to:
```
self.plugin_folder = os.path.abspath(os.path.join(self.app.root_path, plugin_folder))
```
Then user can use relative path for plugin forlder. For example:
```
plugin_manager.init_app(app, plugin_folder="../my_plugins")
```
Best regards,
Andrey.

issue_title: DesignSpaceDocumentReader.process makeKerning relies doc.process makeGlyphs=True
issue_body:
The `makeKerning` parameter in DesignSpaceDocumentReader.process() makes decisions based on which glyphs are present in the target UFO. These glyphs are only generated if the `makeGlyphs=True`. So when testing (for instance) with `makeGlyphs=False` unexpected results might happen.

https://github.com/LettError/MutatorMath/blob/master/Lib/mutatorMath/ufo/instance.py#L72

This would only be an issue when you're generating kerning but not glyphs. So, not sure if it is something that needs immediate fixing. I just wanted to document the dependency here.

issue_title: Unicode 10.0: Vertical_Orientation (vo) support
issue_body:
This would be available for Python3.7.  It appears fairly straight forward with `U`, `R`, `Tu` and `Tr` being the property values for `Vertical_Orientation` or `vo`.  From what I gather, there are characters explicitly assigned to `R`, but anything not defined in `VerticalOrientation.txt` would be assumed `R`.

```
# Vertical_Orientation (vo) Property
#
# This file defines the Vertical_Orientation property. See UAX #50:
# Unicode Vertical Text Layout, at http://www.unicode.org/reports/tr50/
#
# The format of the file is two fields separated by a semicolon.
# Field 0: Unicode code point value or range of code point values in
#   hexadecimal form
# Field 1: Vertical_Orientation property value, one of the following:
#   U - Upright, the same orientation as in the code charts
#   R - Rotated 90 degrees clockwise compared to the code charts
#   Tu - Transformed typographically, with fallback to Upright
#   Tr - Transformed typographically, with fallback to Rotated
#  - All other code points, assigned and unassigned, that are not listed
#      explicitly in the data section of this file are given the value R.
```

issue_title: Admins list isn't read correctly
issue_body:
I've been working on hooking up Celery with Pyramid and ran into what I think is a limitation of the ini-based configuration.

To get error-mails sent when a task fails, I need to set the `ADMINS` list. However, adding an admins variable in the [celery] config section makes this a string rather than the list of 2-tuples that Celery wants. Adding it to the `list_settings` list also doesn't work, as that makes a flat list, which is not accepted.

Extending the `read_configuration()` method of the `INILoader` class with the following (after processing list_settings) does the trick, though I'm not sure it's the best possible solution.

```python
# Following after list_settings parsing

tuple_list_settings = ['ADMINS']

for setting in tuple_list_settings:
    if setting in config_dict:
        items = config_dict[setting].split()
        tuple_settings = [tuple(item.split(',')) for item in items]
        config_dict[setting] = tuple_settings
```

The corresponding ini file would look like this:

```ini
[celery]
admins =
    development,development@example.com
    excecptions,exceptions@example.com
```

issue_title: "LANG=C wormhole receive" fails with a traceback on python 3
issue_body:
as noted over on https://bugs.debian.org/848508, with python3:

```
  joey@darkstar:~>LANG=C wormhole receive
  Traceback (most recent call last):
    File "/usr/bin/wormhole", line 11, in <module>
      load_entry_point('magic-wormhole==0.8.1', 'console_scripts', 'wormhole')()
    File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/click/core.py", line 716, in __call__
      return self.main(*args, **kwargs)
    File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/click/core.py", line 675, in main
      _verify_python3_env()
    File "/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/click/_unicodefun.py", line 119, in _verify_python3_env
      'mitigation steps.' + extra)
  RuntimeError: Click will abort further execution because Python 3 was configured to use ASCII as encoding for the environment.  Either run this under Python 2 or consult http://click.pocoo.org/python3/ for mitigation steps.
```
It would be a shame for magic-wormhole in debian to drop back to python 2 -- i'd prefer to keep things in python 3 where possible.  It's also not clear what wormhole *should* do in an environment that is not unicode-capable, given that the wordlists available to it might well be unicode.

C.UTF-8 apparently isn't available on Mac OS :/

C.UTF-8 should be a strict superset of C as a locale, though.  What if magic-wormhole was just to special-case this situation to work around Click's pre-emptive abort?

issue_title: POINT shapefiles require dataset extent to be drawn in 2.0.0-dev
issue_body:
When creating a POINT shapefile, the `__shapefileHeader()` method is supplying the file header with a unpacked `*self.bbox()` of `0, 0, 0, 0`. What this results in is geometry that has no spatial index. This occurs due to the `self.__bbox()` method never getting called for shapes of type `1` in the `__shpRecord()` method. The `Writer` instance has a `self._bbox` initialized to `[0, 0, 0, 0]` and without calling it ever again, the `save()` method will ultimately try and pack the extent of a point in the file header as `0, 0, 0, 0`. ArcGIS is unable to plot points since no extent is provided, only when another file with an extent is loaded will the points display.

To resolve this (temporarily on a local copy) I've done the following:

    def point(self, x, y, z=0, m=0, shapeType=POINT):
        """Creates a point shape."""
        pointShape = Shape(shapeType)
        if shapeType == POINT:
            pointShape.points.append([x, y])
        elif shapeType == POINTZ:
            pointShape.points.append([x, y, z])
        elif shapeType == POINTM:
            pointShape.points.append([x, y, z, m])
        self.shape(pointShape)
        _ = self.__bbox(pointShape) # catch a throw-away return

All it does is force the global `self._bbox` to update to the extent of the provided points. Since the new implementation of the `self.__bbox()` method updates by comparing the initial `self._bbox()` to the supplied point, the minimum x & y will always be 0 0. For example, if I added a `print()` inside the `__shpRecord()` method when packing the bounding box:

    w = shapefile.Writer(1, True)
    w.field('Point')
    w.record('First')
    w.record('Second')
    w.point(1.0, 2.0)
    w.point(3.0, 4.0)
    w.save('/some/path')
    >>> 0 0 3.0 4.0 # when the 'saveShp()' method calls
    >>> 0 0 3.0 4.0 # when the 'saveShx()' method calls

So in summary, a redesign of how the `self.__bbox()` method is called when dealing with POINTs needs to be performed so that proper extent is written.

issue_title: redis-semaphore is not a distributed semaphore
issue_body:
README says:

> A distributed semaphore and mutex built on Redis.

This isn't true. Creating the "same" semaphore `semaphore = Semaphore(Redis(), count=1, namespace='spam')` in two different threads/processes does not result in `with semaphore: ...` having desired effect (both processes will execute the `with` block together).

It only works as intended when you create a global semaphore shared by two threads like in the [example](https://github.com/bluele/redis-semaphore/blob/f7510c3d1ee551b1f7a7af12b9ce7c52efefe995/examples/semaphore.py). Needless to say redis is not required to achieve the result redis-semaphore achieves.

> close(self)
> Close the transport. If the transport has a buffer for outgoing data, buffered data will be flushed asynchronously. No more data will be received. After all buffered data is flushed, the protocol’s connection_lost() method will be called with None as its argument.

issue_title: util.get_int() broken on Linux / Python 2.7.3
issue_body:
When sending a Logo8 counter-value via network-analog-output to a snap7-server
the values returned from  util.get_int() were not always correct.
The problem is also reproducible with util.set_int() and util.get_int() :

```
import snap7
server = snap7.server.Server(log=True)
DB1 = (snap7.snap7types.wordlen_to_ctypes[snap7.snap7types.S7WLByte]*4)()
server.register_area(snap7.snap7types.srvAreaDB, 1, DB1)
for Data_Set in (-32768, -16385, -256, -128, -127, 0, 127, 128, 255, 256, 16384, 32767):
    snap7.util.set_int(DB1, 0, Data_Set)
    Data_Get = snap7.util.get_int(DB1, 0)
    print "%6d %6d %s" % (Data_Set, Data_Get, (Data_Set==Data_Get))

Output:

 -32768 -32768 True
 -16385 -16641 False
   -256   -256 True
   -128   -384 False
   -127   -383 False
      0      0 True
    127    127 True
    128   -128 False
    255     -1 False
    256    256 True
  16384  16384 True
  32767  32511 False
```

The problem is due to python sign handling of integers.
A working fix in util.get_int():
```
...
    byte1 = _bytearray[byte_index + 1] & 0xFF

Output:

-32768 -32768 True
-16385 -16385 True
  -256   -256 True
  -128   -128 True
  -127   -127 True
     0      0 True
   127    127 True
   128    128 True
   255    255 True
   256    256 True
 16384  16384 True
 32767  32767 True
```

byte0 is left with sign, because Logo int ranges from -32768 ... +32767

issue_title: Temporary user backend
issue_body:
Is there any existing backend to support a "temporary" user? This isn't a good word for it -- let me explain what I'm looking for:

While I want information a given user enters to be stored in a user record, and for them to be treated as if they are logged in, I don't want to require them to log in with a social account or to make a password. They'd be remembered as logged in, and so associated to their row in the users table for now, via session or cookie, and they have the option while still logged in to become associated with an password or a social account so they can log back in later. If they don't do this their record would still be stored but they (probably?) wouldn't be able to become reassociated with it at a later time.

I suspect I may be able to use the "email" backend for this and just not set a password, since I'll be collecting email addresses anyway. What I'm not clear on is how feasible this is, and whether I'd then easily the user would be able to add a password or associate a social account with the already-logged-in account.

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

issue_title: For all users trying to install ERAlchemy on Windows 10.
issue_body:
After much effort I gave up and installed:

> Bash on Ubuntu on Windows

1. Open PowerShell as Administrator and run:
PS:> `Enable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName Microsoft-Windows-Subsystem-Linux`
2. Restart your computer when prompted.
3. Install [Ubuntu](https://www.microsoft.com/store/p/ubuntu/9nblggh4msv6):

More info @ https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/wsl/install-win10.

Next open up your new bash terminal (Start > bash).
Then run your basic update all command. 
1. $`sudo apt-get update`  https://askubuntu.com/a/196777/793432.
2. $`sudo apt-get install python-2.7 python-pip`
3. $`sudo pip install graphviz`
4. $`sudo pip install eralchemy`
5. $`sudo pip install SQLAlchemy`

You will also need to install the python driver for your database.
eg: `sudo pip install mysql-python` for MySQL driver. See http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/dialects/mysql.html#dialect-mysql
And you will need to install MySQL (if you are using that).
eg: `sudo apt-get install mysql-server`
See https://dev.mysql.com/doc/mysql-apt-repo-quick-guide/en/

And you will probably need to set up the MySQL database just like you would for Windows.

If you are missing any modules I recommend googling for them like so:

> apt-get install some-missing-module ubuntu

issue_title: there might be a bug
issue_body:
I got some exception while use kafka-python

```python
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/kafka/selectors34.py", line 340, in select
    r, w, _ = self._select(self._readers, self._writers, [], timeout)
TypeError: select() takes at most 4 arguments (5 given)
```

```python
    if sys.platform == 'win32':
        def _select(self, r, w, _, timeout=None):
            r, w, x = select.select(r, w, w, timeout)
            return r, w + x, []
    else:
        _select = select.select
```

If the sys.platform is not 'win32', `self._select` will be `select.select`

and there will add one more argument `self`

there can use `staticmethod` like this to reslove this problem



```python
    if sys.platform == 'win32':
        @staticmethod
        def _select(r, w, _, timeout=None):
            r, w, x = select.select(r, w, w, timeout)
            return r, w + x, []
    else:
        _select = staticmethod(select.select)
```

hope your reply.

issue_title: Custom SQLA types
issue_body:
For classes which are inherited from `sqlalchemy.types.TypeDecorator`.

For example, the following snippet for a `ChoiceType` class is persisted as `VARCHAR(1)`.
```python
class ChoiceType(TypeDecorator):
    impl = sqlalchemy.types.String(length=1)

    def __init__(self, choices, **kw):
        self.choices = dict(choices)
        super(ChoiceType, self).__init__(**kw)

    def process_bind_param(self, value, dialect):
        return [unicode(k) for k, v in self.choices.iteritems()
                if v == value][0]

    def process_result_value(self, value, dialect):
        return self.choices[value]
```

The parser throws an `InvalidStatus` exception:
```python
$ENV/lib/python2.7/site-packages/alchemyjsonschema/__init__.pyc in __getitem__(self, k)
    107             if issubclass(cls, type_):
    108                 return type_, self.mapping[type_]
--> 109         raise InvalidStatus("notfound: {k}".format(k=k))
    110
    111 DefaultClassfier = Classifier(default_column_to_schema)

InvalidStatus: notfound: VARCHAR(1) 
```

I believe, in this particular case of `VARCHAR(n)`, it should be converted to:
```json
{ 
    "type": "string",
    "maxLength": <<LENGTH>>,
}
```
